title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about mental health and wellbeing in modern society. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Quiet Epidemic
content.subtitle: An article exploring changing attitudes towards mental health.
content.text:
A generation ago, admitting to anxiety or depression in a professional setting was likely to carry serious consequences for a person's career. Today, the conversation has shifted — at least on the surface. Awareness campaigns fill social media feeds, corporations announce wellbeing initiatives, and celebrities speak openly about their struggles with mental illness. [31] Yet many mental health professionals caution that visibility alone does not equal understanding, and that a culture which celebrates the language of mental health while failing to fund treatment is guilty of a particular kind of hypocrisy. [/31]

The numbers tell a sobering story. In many high-income countries, waiting times for psychological therapies can stretch to months or even years, while demand continues to rise. [32] Young people appear especially affected; rates of anxiety and depression among adolescents have climbed steadily over the past two decades, a trend that predates social media, though the latter is often blamed as the primary cause. [/32] Researchers point instead to a more complex web of factors: academic pressure, economic uncertainty, reduced time spent outdoors, and the fragmentation of traditional community structures.

One of the most contested questions in the field concerns how mental health conditions should be understood. The medical model — which treats conditions such as depression as biological disorders to be corrected with medication — has been both enormously helpful and heavily criticised. [33] Proponents argue that framing mental illness as a brain disorder reduces stigma by removing moral judgement. Opponents counter that it risks overlooking the social and environmental causes that are often just as significant as any neurological factor. [/33]

Therapy itself is also evolving. Alongside traditional talking therapies, practitioners are exploring approaches that engage the body as well as the mind. [34] Activities such as cold-water swimming, forest bathing, and community gardening have attracted interest from researchers, some of whom report measurable improvements in mood and a reduction in symptoms of anxiety. [/34] These approaches are unlikely to replace conventional treatment, but they point to a broadening understanding of what recovery might look like.

Workplace wellbeing remains a particularly thorny area. While many employers now offer employee assistance programmes and mental health days, critics argue that these measures address the symptoms of an unhealthy work culture rather than the causes. [35] Offering a mindfulness app to someone who is overworked and undervalued, the argument goes, is like providing plasters to someone who keeps walking into walls. [/35] True progress, according to this view, requires structural change: fairer workloads, greater job security, and a culture that does not equate long hours with commitment.

Ultimately, the conversation about mental health is still finding its shape. [36] What seems clear is that the answer will require not just clinical innovation but a willingness to examine the conditions in which people live and work — and to ask uncomfortable questions about what it means to be well in the contemporary world. [/36]

questions.question: What criticism do mental health professionals make of current public attitudes?
options: A) People discuss mental health too openly on social media.
B) Celebrities give inaccurate information about mental illness.
C) Awareness of mental health has not been matched by adequate support.
D) Corporations are exploiting mental health campaigns for marketing.
questions.explanation: Professionals warn that 'visibility alone does not equal understanding' and criticise a culture that promotes mental health language without funding treatment.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about the rise in mental health problems among young people?
options: A) Social media is the main reason for the increase.
B) The causes are more varied than is commonly assumed.
C) Academic pressure is the single most important factor.
D) The trend only became noticeable after the rise of social media.
questions.explanation: The author says the trend 'predates social media' and researchers identify 'a more complex web of factors', indicating causes are more varied than the commonly blamed social media.

questions.question: The debate about the medical model of mental illness centres on whether
options: A) medication is more effective than therapy for all conditions.
B) biological explanations adequately account for all aspects of mental illness.
C) doctors are qualified to treat conditions that are social in origin.
D) reducing stigma is the most important goal of mental health treatment.
questions.explanation: Opponents argue the medical model 'risks overlooking the social and environmental causes', suggesting biological explanations are not sufficient on their own.

questions.question: The author mentions cold-water swimming and community gardening to illustrate
options: A) how mental health treatment is moving away from talking therapies.
B) that physical activity is more effective than medication.
C) a growing interest in non-traditional approaches to mental health recovery.
D) the importance of spending time outdoors for general health.
questions.explanation: These activities are cited as examples of practitioners 'exploring approaches that engage the body as well as the mind', illustrating a broadening of treatment approaches.

questions.question: The comparison to 'providing plasters to someone who keeps walking into walls' suggests that
options: A) employee wellbeing programmes are completely ineffective.
B) mental health apps are not designed for serious conditions.
C) workplace wellbeing measures treat effects rather than underlying causes.
D) overworked employees are responsible for their own mental health.
questions.explanation: The analogy is used to argue that workplace wellbeing initiatives 'address the symptoms of an unhealthy work culture rather than the causes'.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author implies that improving mental health requires
options: A) significant advances in neuroscience and pharmaceutical research.
B) a broader approach that includes examining social and working conditions.
C) greater openness from public figures about their personal struggles.
D) international cooperation to share best practice in mental healthcare.
questions.explanation: The author calls for 'a willingness to examine the conditions in which people live and work', indicating a broader social approach alongside clinical innovation.
